narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 77% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its students. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your class rankings from your first and third undergraduate years, where you placed in the lowest decile both times—far below the top-half positions typical of students who go on to pass the bar. Your third-year decile rank stood out as the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signaled to the model a persistent struggle with coursework that often foreshadows bar challenges. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 24, which is substantially lower than the average of about 37 for those who pass, further reinforcing the model's concerns about your readiness for the rigors ahead. Your first-year decile rank in the bottom tier added to this, contributing another notable push toward the failure prediction and highlighting early academic hurdles that the model sees as hard to overcome. While factors like your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the 3.24 average for passers), low family income bracket, and choice to attend full-time were noted, they played smaller roles compared to these top three. In contrast, admitted students typically have much stronger showings in class deciles and LSAT scores, often landing in the upper half of their classes with scores well above 35, setting them apart as low-risk for bar failure. Your profile, while showing determination through full-time commitment, aligned more closely with patterns the model associates with higher bar risks. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades in any further coursework to climb those class rankings, and exploring targeted bar prep resources early. With focused improvements in these areas, you could reapply stronger and shift the model's view in your favor. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key predictors head-on.
